mainEntity.name: Can I get workers comp for a hernia if I got it from lifting at my job?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, hernias caused by workplace lifting are covered. However, you must report the specific incident and seek medical care quickly, as insurance companies often claim hernias are "pre-existing" or non-work-related if there is a delay in reporting.
acceptedAnswer.description:
Yes, a hernia is one of the most common overexertion claims in Idaho and Utah. However, insurance carriers scrutinize hernias heavily. To get it covered, you usually need to show that the hernia appeared suddenly after a specific lifting incident or "strain," and that you sought medical attention shortly thereafter. 

If you had a hernia years ago that was repaired, and this is a new one in the same spot, it is still a new work injury.

Key evidence includes the "history" you give the ER or clinic doctor—it must be consistent with workplace exertion.

Report the incident to your boss before the end of your shift. Go to an urgent care or your designated company doctor and ensure "work-related lifting" is written in the chart. Dealing with the specific "hernia rules" in work comp often requires legal guidance to ensure the medical evidence meets the state's strict criteria.
